premises.content: The alignment assumption underlying the DGM (that task-solving skills are the same as self-modification skills) is unlikely to hold outside coding domains, where task-solving skills may differ substantially from the skills needed to analyze failures, propose effective self-improvements, and implement them.
premises.label: alignment_assumption_limits_dgm

premises.content: On the Polyglot coding benchmark (50-task training subset), the initial agent achieves a training score of 0.140 and a full-benchmark score of 0.084.
premises.label: coding_initial_score

premises.content: The original DGM (Zhang et al., 2025) achieves coding improvements from 0.140 to 0.380 on the 50-task Polyglot training subset and from 0.142 to 0.307 on the full Polyglot benchmark, using a handcrafted instruction-generation mechanism specifically designed for coding.
premises.label: dgm_coding_result

premises.content: The Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM) relies on a handcrafted, fixed mechanism to produce self-improvement instructions. This mechanism is not modifiable. The DGM's capacity for self-improvement is therefore bottlenecked by this fixed instruction-generation step. The DGM's ability to improve at self-improving depends on an alignment assumption: that the skills required to solve evaluation tasks are the same as those required for effective self-reflection and self-modification.
premises.label: dgm_limitation

premises.content: The original DGM (designed for coding), when applied without domain customization, achieves paper review test-set performance of 0.0 (CI: 0.0–0.510). When manually customized (DGM-custom), it achieves 0.590 (CI: 0.570–0.650).
premises.label: dgm_paper_review_result

premises.content: Transfer agents from DGM-custom runs achieve imp@50 ≈ 0.0 (CI: 0.0–0.010) on Olympiad-level math grading when using DGM w/o self-improve as the generation algorithm. This shows DGM-custom does not learn transferable meta-level improvements.
premises.label: dgm_transfer_imp50

premises.content: On the Polyglot coding benchmark, DGM-H improves its training score on the 50-task subset from 0.140 (initial agent) to 0.340 (CI: 0.300–0.380) over 80 iterations. On the full Polyglot benchmark (largely unseen tasks), performance increases from 0.084 (initial) to 0.267 (CI: 0.231–0.280).
premises.label: dgmh_coding_result

premises.content: DGM-H run on Olympiad-level math grading, initialized from transfer hyperagents, achieves faster progress and higher final performance compared to DGM-H initialized from the initial agent, indicating previously learned self-improvements remain useful and compound in a new domain.
premises.label: dgmh_compound_faster

premises.content: DGM-Hyperagents (DGM-H) augments the original DGM with hyperagents. DGM-H employs the open-ended exploration process in the DGM (archive, parent selection, evaluation) while adding metacognitive self-modification: the meta agent that generates modifications is itself part of the editable hyperagent codebase and can be modified.
premises.label: dgmh_extends_dgm

premises.content: DGM-H initialized with ProofAutoGrader as the task agent and a transfer meta agent from prior DGM-H runs achieves a test-set score of 0.700 on Olympiad-level math grading after 200 iterations, compared to ProofAutoGrader's score of 0.670. On the full IMO-GradingBench, DGM-H improves accuracy from 0.561 to 0.601 and reduces mean absolute error from 0.178 to 0.175.
premises.label: dgmh_proofautograder_result

premises.content: Transfer hyperagents from prior DGM-H runs on paper review + robotics reward design achieve initial test-set score of 0.0 (CI: 0.0–0.0) on Olympiad-level math grading, reflecting the domain shift.
premises.label: dgmh_transfer_agents_score

premises.content: DGM-H without self-improving meta agents (DGM-H w/o self-improve), which fixes the meta agent throughout the run (replicating the ADAS approach), achieves test-set performance of 0.0 (CI: 0.0–0.130) on paper review and 0.213 (CI: 0.180–0.348) on robotics reward design. DGM-H significantly outperforms this baseline (p < 0.05) in both domains.
premises.label: dgmh_wout_self_improve

premises.content: On paper review (test set), the initial agent achieves a score of 0.0, because it fails to produce outputs in the required format due to the absence of task-specific parsing or post-processing.
premises.label: paper_review_initial

premises.content: DGM without domain customization predicts paper review performance ~0.0 on test, because its fixed instruction-generation mechanism is not suited to non-coding tasks.
premises.label: pred_dgm_paper

premises.content: DGM-custom predicts that task-level improvements transfer, but meta-level improvements do not, so imp@50 ≈ 0 when starting from DGM-custom transfer agents.
premises.label: pred_dgm_transfer

premises.content: DGM-H (metacognitive self-modification) predicts paper review performance of ~0.710 on test.
premises.label: pred_dgmh_paper

premises.content: DGM-H predicts that hyperagents encode transferable self-improvement mechanisms enabling imp@50 > 0 in a new domain when starting from transfer hyperagents.
premises.label: pred_dgmh_transfer
